results.title: I tried viral fizzy drink Poppi after UK launch and 1 thing really took me by surprise - The Mirror
results.author: Alice Sjoberg
results.text:
I tried viral fizzy drink Poppi after UK launch and 1 thing really took me by surprise - The Mirror

‌

‌

# I tried viral fizzy drink Poppi after UK launch and 1 thing really took me by surprise

## Social News Reporter Alice Sjoberg tried the viral Poppi fizzy drinks, which have recently launched in the UK after having gone viral across the US. But are they as good as people say?

Comments

Alice Sjoberg Social News Reporter

10:08, 14 Mar 2026Updated 10:31, 14 Mar 2026

View 2 Images

Social News reporter Alice got to try the viral drinks(Image: Alice Sjoberg)

If you've ever watched TikTok videos, you might have come across people across the US talking about a fizzy drink brand called Poppi. It's been a hit for celebrities such as Hailey Bieber, Kylie Jenner, and Jennerfer Lopez, who have all been spotted enjoying the drink, which claims to be a 'better version' of fizzy drinks.

‌

The drinks launched in the UK in March 2026, specifically in Tesco and Pret, which has seen shoppers rush to get their hands on the popular drink - including me. I, personally, was intrigued by the fact the company described the drinks as flavoured fizzy water, which is my favourite kind of drink, so I was excited to give it a go.

‌

On first impression, the colourful cans immediately made me smile. Each flavour has a cute illustration to show the flavour of the drink, while the colour for the can compliments the fruit or berry in question.

‌

I then noticed the ingredients and content values, which really took me by surprise. Each drink are made with fizzy water and natural flavouring. They also only contain five grams of sugar and 30 calories per 330ml can, which is great as other fizzy drinks, such as a can of Coca Cola contain 39 grams of sugar and 139 calories for the same amount.

Each Poppi also contains prebiotics, which are a special type of fibre that can act as food for healthy bacteria in your gut, as well as apple cider vinegar, which the brand claims gives the drinks 'a mouthwatering edge'. But how true is this? Let's put them to the test...

### Strawberry Lemon

Out of the selection of flavours, the Strawberry Lemon flavour was the one I was the most excited to try. As soon as I cracked the can open, I could smell the sweet and fruity scent coming from the drink, immediately reminding me of summer.

‌

And the flavour didn't disappoint either. My immediately thought would that it would most likely become my go-to summer drink, as it would be great either on is own, or as a mixer with gin.

However, the aftertaste surprised me, as it tasted a bit... artificial? Looking at the ingredient list, which also surprised me by how good it looked compared to normal fizzy drinks, I soon came to the conclusion the flavour came from the stevia added to it.

As I'm not used to drinking drinks with artificial sweeteners, it took me a bit to get used to, but by the time I finished my glass, I was back to fully enjoying the drink. I will absolutely be getting thi

results.title: Poppi Prebiotic Soda Review (2024) | The Quality Edit
results.text:
Poppi Prebiotic Soda Review (2024) | The Quality Edit 

 

/

Home

/

Food & Beverage

# Poppi Soda Review: Is TikTok's Favorite Prebiotic Soda All It's Cracked Up To Be?

Words by

##### The Editors

Share

Published on:

Jul 11, 2023

Mar 7, 2024

Credit: @drinkpoppi

Our team is dedicated to finding and telling you more about the web’s best products. If you purchase through our links, we may receive a commission. Our editorial team is independent and only endorses products we believe in.

Even if no one has directly lectured you about the importance of getting your daily dose of apple cider vinegar for all the health benefits it offers, you’ve probably started to notice that suddenly it's everywhere. Maybe you're already convinced you need to fold it into your daily routine because of all the probiotic and prebiotic drinks popping up all over Instagram and in your local supermarket. Or perhaps you’ve only heard of probiotics and don’t even know what prebiotics are.

Allison Ellsworth's journey started in a similar place, until she found that consuming apple cider vinegar daily helped heal several of her chronic health problems. But as grateful as she was for its effects, that didn't mean she adjusted very easily to the taste. After experimenting with several fresh fruit juice and ACV combinations, Ellsworth and her husband landed on what would become the recipe for Poppi, their line of sparkling prebiotic sodas making it easy to down about a tablespoon of ACV in a delicious fizzy drink. And having tried it out recently, I can see why it's been such an overnight success.

#### We like

- Poppi’s wide range of flavors are actually delicious and serve as a healthy party beverage for anyone wanting a non-alcoholic drink.
- It’s available in most grocery stores. Check out Poppi’s store locator to find your nearest Poppi!

#### We don't like

- As with everything, moderation is key. Though it’s tempting to have more than a couple of Poppis a day, we advise against that for the negative effects of consuming too much ACV.

### Featured Product

Poppi

### Cherry Limeade

$29.88/12-Pack

$29.88/12-Pack

Code:

Shop Now

## Cheers to gut health

So how exactly does Poppi help out your gut? Poppi is made using an organic apple cider vinegar as well as some of the “mother,” or the byproduction of bacteria and yeast used in ACV production that are beneficial to gut health. (If that sounds off-putting, know that kombucha goes through a similar process.) On top of that, the sodas contain polyphenols and prebiotics from pectin, a complex starch derived from plants that has been shown to regulate intestinal inflammation. ACV can help improve digestion by raising the stomach’s acidity levels, and faster digestion means you experience less gas and bloating after ingesting a meal.

Credit:@drinkpoppi

Some of the other ACV benefits touted on Poppi’s site are that the soda may help lower bad cholesterol while increasing good cholesterol; it may help stabilize blo

results.title: Poppi and Olipop: A Soda Dupe?
results.author:
Hanna Brown 
																			 Student Contributor, The University of Kansas
results.text:
Poppi and Olipop: A Soda Dupe?

More from KU

- Our Reads
- Meet the Team
- Join Us

Jocelyn Hsu / Spoon

# Poppi and Olipop: A Soda Dupe?

We are in the midst of a fun drink culture, all thanks to TikTok. This is not just a phenomenon thats hits the younger generations but the older ones as well. I think we all remember water tok. For those of us not putting Skittles flavoring and Skinny Syrup in our water, how does the trend affect us? Well, let me introduce you to the world of soda alternatives.

Two companies come to mind when thinking about soda alternatives, Poppi and Olipop. Both brands market their products as healthy versions of soda that target gut health. As a self-proclaimed soda girly, I felt compelled to try these alternatives and give you my honest opinion and if they are Hanna-approved. First on the try list is Olipop.

## Olipop

I’m more familiar with Olipop. I remember seeing it on shelves years ago and wanting to try their Minion banana cream soda. On the outside, this brand is more appealing to me. I love their can designs and they seem more like a soda alternative than a health drink. Additionally, as a soda drinker, they have several flavors that mimic my favs, like Doctor Goodwin, Vintage Cola and Classic Rootbeer.

For my taste test, I picked two off the shelves at Walmart for $2.49 each. The flavors I chose were Classic Rootbeer and Orange Squeeze. Right away, I noticed the carbonation was off. While they still have some fizz, do not expect a McDonald’s level carbonation. The flavors also had an aftertaste I can’t pin. I preferred the Classic Rootbeer over the Orange Squeeze. The rootbeer did not taste like A&W, but it did taste like those fancy ones you get at Whole Foods.

View this post on Instagram

A post shared by OLIPOP (@drinkolipop)

## Poppi

If it was not for one of my friends being a diehard Poppi fan, I probably wouldn’t have tried it. I think their branding is gorgeous and their social media is peak, but something about their product doesn’t appeal to me. It feels almost unattainable. When I picture a Poppi drinker, I see a gorgeous tan-fit pilates goddess and that’s just not me. Not to mention it’s unavailable at budget stores, like Walmart.

I grabbed Rasberry Rose, Strawberry Lemon and Ginger Lime. I know they have classic flavors, like Doc Pop, but unfortunately, they were sold out at my Target. Right off the bat, I did not like it as a soda alternative. The product did not scream soda, the carbonation was lacking and it tasted too much like apple cider vinegar. I think it is a good health drink, but marketing it as a soda dupe doesn’t hit the mark.

View this post on Instagram

A post shared by Poppi (@drinkpoppi)

## In-Conclusion

Overall, these are good products. I can see the appeal and even liked some of the flavors, but they are not the soda dupes people make them out to be. If you want a soda alternative, I would recommend Olipop over Poppi. However, do not expect it to fill the soda-sized hole

results.title: A New Type of Soda: A Review of Poppi
results.author:
Helen Velazquez 
																			 Student Contributor, Manhattan College
results.text:
A New Type of Soda: A Review of Poppi

HC at Manhattan

More from Manhattan

- Join Us
- Meet the Team
- Our Reads

Photo by Spencer Davis from Unsplash

Manhattan| Wellness Health

# A New Type of Soda: A Review of Poppi

Soda, Pop, Coke: the beloved carbonated drink takes up many different names. However, a new one that may have to be added to the list is Poppi. It sounds like the name of what a little fairy in a Barbie movie may be named. Poppi is advertised to have only five grams of sugar, contain less than 25 calories, and have prebiotics. It sounds almost too good to be true. The main prebiotic in Poppi is apple cider vinegar, which has polyphenols, a source of antioxidants. Polyphenols are also found in blueberries, flaxseeds, artichokes, and olives, which are all major health foods. The official Poppi website is very transparent about everything that goes into one of their delicious cans. Instead of high fructose corn syrup, Poppi uses cane sugar and organic agave inulin to sweeten the drink. Coca-Cola has almost four times as much sugar content in its cans than a Poppi can. The organic agave inulin not only acts as a sweetener but also as a prebiotic fiber. It acts as “fuel” for good bacteria in the gut which causes more to be produced. The major downside is one of the factors that sets Poppi apart from mainstream soda brands, the price. For an astounding $30 you can buy a 12-pack. This is almost twice the price of what a 12-pack of mainstream soda would be. Additionally, while this may be a healthier alternative to soda, it most definitely should not replace the actual fruits, vegetables, and yogurts that can naturally provide prebiotics into your diet.

Now that we have a background of what Poppi is and how it is different from your typical soda, the question that has to be answered is what does it taste like?

1. Orange: 5/10

Perhaps I rated this so low because I am not usually a fan of orange flavorings, not even orange juice really, but there was something that I just could not love about the flavor profile of this soda. I wanted more out of it but it just tasted like a typical artificial orange soft drink (nothing spectacular).

1. Doc Pop: 8/10

One of my favorite sodas is Doctor Pepper, my go-to for years, so Poppi had to work to impress my taste buds. I think Poppi does a good job as a dupe, but it has a more healthy and organic taste which is not necessarily a bad thing. I think that the “healthier” and lighter taste it has actually works and gives a different flavor that an avid doctor pepper lover would enjoy.

1. Grape: 9/10

Poppi’s grape soda really stood out to me and has even made me start craving grape soda. Poppi’s grape surprisingly tastes very fresh and is quite refreshing, which I think can be attributed to the actual grape juice they use. Poppi’s grape soda has grown on me and I think could be liked by those who do not usually find themselves grabbing a grape soda from the vending machine.

1. Classic Cola: 7/10

results.title: The Ultimate Poppi Review: A Healthier Soda
results.text:
-- The Ultimate Poppi Review: A Healthier Soda

top of page

Search

A common New Year’s resolution is the desire to be healthier in one’s daily life. Health trends are very popular on social media as people love sharing and finding little tips and lifestyles that help them to prioritize their health. One of the most popular trends in recent years has been taking foods that a person typically consumes and swapping them out for healthier alternatives that taste similar. From small snacks to full meals, if there’s a food you like, there’s an influencer on social media with a “healthier” version of it. One of the most popular alternatives in recent years has been with soda. Although many well-known soda brands have diet versions of their drinks, many people are still not satisfied with the options presented to them, so they may choose to search for brands that specialize in creating healthier sodas. Poppi is a soda company that creates soda with probiotics and apple cider vinegar. The brand has been on Shark Tank and has been featured by influencers and celebrities, but is it really as good as normal soda? We’re here to find out.

As loyal Whole Foods and Sprouts shoppers, we are no strangers to all sorts of healthy alternatives and probiotic drinks. Seeing the recent rise in Poppi’s popularity, we set out to review these fizzy drinks that promised to be oh-so-good for our gut health. And yes, we looked like maniacs carrying every flavor to the checkout line at the grocery store. It was worth it though, for now we are qualified Poppi experts. However, our stomachs may have actually hurt more than before after drinking this much soda (how ironic!).... Here are our reviews of the fourteen flavors, as well as a review of the “ultimate” Poppi.

Photo provided by authors.

Photo provided by authors.

Strawberry Lemon (7.66/10)

This flavor is absolutely delicious. It tastes just like strawberry lemonade with a fizzy twist and does not taste like a health drink at all. We all drank strawberry lemonade as kids, which makes this Poppi perfect to drink if you’re feeling a bit nostalgic for the classic drink.

Orange (7/10)

Orange was good, but didn’t stand out as great. It tastes just like a Fanta with less bubbles and sugar in it. I would rather just drink a Fanta, honestly.

Raspberry Rose (9/10)

This flavor is definitely the best. First of all, it’s just so cute– the baby pink, smell of rose, and gentle taste of raspberry. It’s also perfectly romantic and makes one happy to be drinking it. As raspberry enthusiasts, we gladly report that the flavor of raspberry itself is spot on, almost reminiscent of real raspberry.

Wild Berry (8/10)

Wild berry is most well-known for being featured in Poppi’s partnership with Post Malone and it is certainly deserving of this spotlight. The only flaw of this drink would be its sweetness, but that also makes it taste more like regular soda. It tastes very similar to a Blue Raspberry Slurpee at the movie theatres; so if

results.title: Here's What Two Dietitians Have To Say About Poppi And Other Prebiotic Sodas So You Can Make Your Own Informed Decision
results.text:
Here's What Two Dietitians Have To Say About Poppi And Other Prebiotic Sodas So You Can Make Your Own Informed Decision

Advertisement

Advertisement

## Trending:

- Best Amazon deals
- Best-tested under-$300 robot vacuum
- Top sales this week
- This $25 Old Navy dress is the best I tried
- Best pillows for neck pain

## It's hard to have missed the recent backlash that the prebiotic soda brand Poppi has been facing after sending full-size vending machines to influencers around the US.

The key insight from the controversy has sparked discussions about customer-first marketing, emphasizing the importance of prioritizing the everyday consumer's experience over creating exclusive opportunities for wealthy influencers. Social media consultant Rachel Karten has an interesting take — you can read more about her perspective here.

## That being said, prebiotic and probiotic drinks are here to stay — and Poppi is continuing to make their presence known with big sales and displays like this:

Krista Torres

## When it comes to the products themselves, we are seeing one common theme people want to know: Just how good are these sodas for you?

## It's no secret the term "gut healthy," previously used in Poppi's marketing, has raised questions (the brand has an ongoing lawsuit for consumer fraud and false and misleading advertising — one they claim is baseless).

Courtneyk / Getty Images

Advertisement

Advertisement

## So, to answer some of our questions, BuzzFeed spoke to Registered Dietician at Orlando Health, Shannon O’Meara, MS, RD, LDN — and Registered Dietician and Content Creator Taylor Grasso, MPPD, RD, LD, who owns Simply Healthy Nutrition and hosts her podcast Simply Balanced.

Courtesy Shannon O’Meara, Laura Wills Photography / instagram.com/ Instagram: @laurawillsphotography

## To start off, Grasso explained that it's important to understand the difference between prebiotics and probiotics. "A prebiotic is a type of fiber, while probiotics are a type of bacteria. Prebiotics are important because they actually 'feed' probiotics. Probiotics are considered beneficial bacteria that exist in our gut microbiome, and it's important to consume probiotic fibers to help increase and feed those beneficial probiotics in the gut."

O’Meara added, "Probiotics are typically found in fermented foods like kimchi, sauerkraut, kombucha, or even sourdough. Prebiotics are typically found in high-fiber foods like fruits and vegetables. A common source of prebiotics people probably consume on a daily basis include onions and garlic!"

Krista Torres

## So, let's get into these "gut-healthy" drinks. Grasso said that even though Poppi and Olipop are both prebiotic sodas, it is kind of like comparing apples to oranges. "Each drink is so different. For Poppi, there isn't as much nutritional benefit, but overall, it's a much healthier alternative to a sugary soda. Olipop does contain a proprietary fiber blend that gets you up to nine grams of fiber per can." Grasso has

results.title: Is Poppi Good For You? Dietitians Explain Gut Health Claims And ...
results.text:
Is Poppi Good For You? Dietitians Explain Gut Health Claims And Lawsuit

A $5 Makeup Multi-Stick and More Picks Our Editors Never Leave Home Without

- Add TODAY to Google

Meet Allison Ellsworth, the founder of the soda company Poppi

04:08

Add TODAY to Google

As better-for-you sodas continue to pop up, they're serving as enticing alternatives to traditional sugary drinks. And according to dietitians, when it comes to calories, these soda substitutes made by brands such as Poppi, are superior. The average soda packs around 40 grams of sugar while Poppi only boasts around 5 grams.

The sugar content of the average soda can contribute to weight gain, diabetes and heart disease, TODAY.com previously reported. Plus, it surpasses the daily recommended serving for sugar, which is 25 grams per day for women and 36 grams for men, according to the American Heart Association.

But while these newer options are preferable to the sugary ones, are they living up to all their other promises?

That was the question at the center of a lawsuit filed against Poppi, a prebiotic soda brand, on May 30, 2024, by a woman in San Francisco. In her complaint, she said Poppi’s promises that its prebiotic soda is a good alternative to conventional sugar-loaded sodas are misleading. The product claims to be “‘gut healthy’” due to its inclusion of ‘prebiotics,’” but there’s not enough prebiotic fiber in a single can to produce “meaningful gut health benefits,” she alleged.

Nearly a year later, as parties finalize a settlement agreement, Poppi and alternative soda brands remain popular with consumers. So much so that PepsiCo announced on March 17, 2025, that it would be buying Poppi for $1.95 billion (net price of $1.65 billion), 10 years after Poppi was founded.

Ahead, learn all there is to know about Poppi and prebiotic sodas, and tips if you’re looking for healthier soda options.

## What is prebiotic soda?

“A prebiotic soda is simply a soda that contains prebiotic fibers, with the intent to be gut healthy,” Keri Gans, registered dietitian and author of "The Small Change Diet," told TODAY.com.

Prebiotics are fibers that feed probiotics, the microorganisms living in our intestines, per the Cleveland Clinic. By eating prebiotics, you're helping to fortify the probiotics in your gut, which work to improve digestion, immune health and other bodily functions, TODAY.com previously reported.

In Poppi’s case, the soda is enhanced with a prebiotic fiber called inulin, the only fiber found in the product, according to its website. “Inulin is commonly used in many food and beverage products, likely because it mixes well with other ingredients,” Gans explains.

Inulin naturally occurs in foods including artichokes, asparagus, garlic, soybeans, onions and oats, among many others, according to the Cleveland Clinic. The kind that's used in Poppi comes from the plant agave.

Olipop, another popular prebiotic soda brand, contains 9 grams of prebiotic fiber, including inulin, per can

# These New Shirley Temple Canned Sodas Are Everywhere —I Tried Both to Understand the Hype

Celia FunderburkEditorial Assistant at The Kitchn

Celia Funderburk Editorial Assistant at The Kitchn

I’ve always loved experimenting in journalism, but combining my passions for cooking and writing here at The Kitchn is so rewarding by engaging in trending food culture. With a background in shopping writing at BuzzFeed and Shop TODAY, I also help readers know the best products and deals to shop.

Follow

published Jan 27, 2026

Save

pinterest

Copy URLPage URL

comments

We independently select these products—if you buy from one of our links, we may earn a commission. All prices were accurate at the time of publishing.

Credit: Celia Funderburk

Ah, the Shirley Temple — the original mocktail. This iconic sparkling beverage of ginger ale, grenadine, and maraschino cherries is especially popular among kids (although adults love Dirty Shirleys, too).

Speaking as a former server, I have to admit I used to groan when someone ordered a Shirley Temple. Not only did it mean that I had to leave all of my tables to make the drink in the back, but I also always ended up craving it too. Yes, it may be super sweet, but it’s also ridiculously delicious. So when I saw that prebiotic soda brands Poppi and Olipop both released Shirley Temple flavors, I knew I needed to try them.

Credit: Celia Funderburk

A few weeks back I started seeing post after post about Poppi and Olipop launching their Shirley Temple sodas, and my interest was piqued. I’m not really a big soda or sparkling water drinker to begin with, but I do love an occasional Poppi or Olipop — so I was very excited to try this new flavor.

People are very vocal online about their love of Olipop and Poppi. Many tend to prefer one brand over the other, but I don’t drink them enough to have a favorite. But with both brands launching the same flavor at the same time, I knew I had to find out which one I liked better.

Credit: Celia Funderburk

## What You Should Know About Olipop’s Shirley Temple

Olipop describes their new soda as a combination of “smooth vanilla flavor with bright lemon and lime, finished with cherry juice for that nostalgic grenadine-like flavor.” While it seems to be taking inspiration from a classic Shirley Temple, Olipop’s flavoring is focused on being “bolder, brighter, and better” than the original. They are also leaning into the potentially controversial fourth ingredient of lime, which I personally don’t think belongs in a Shirley Temple.

Olipop now has its own scientific advisory board that conducts human gut microbiome research to ensure Olipop actually does what it claims, which is to create a soda that “supports digestive health.” This flavor has 6 grams of fiber and 4 grams of sugar. The can also states it contains prebiotic
